metadata.source_file: Each 0.5¬įC of Warming Increases Annual Flood Losses in China by More than US$60 Billion.md
metadata.title: Each 0.5¬įC of Warming Increases Annual Flood Losses in China by More than US$60 Billion
metadata.abstract: **ABSTRACT:** In the warming climate, flood risk is likely to increase over much of the globe. We present projections of changes of flood losses in China for a range of global warming scenarios, from 1.5¬į to 4.0¬įC above the preindustrial temperature, with a 0.5¬įC step. Projections of flood losses in China are based on river runoff simulations by a distributed hydrological model driven by multiple downscaled general circulation models, the national GDP projected at shared socioeconomic pathways, and the ‚Äúintensity‚Äďloss rate‚ÄĚ function. When interpreting changes caused by the combined effect of economic and climatic conditions, flood losses in China are projected to soar in the future, particularly in lowland regions subject to rapid economic growth. Under global warming of 1.5¬į and 4.0¬įC, in an average year, flood losses are projected to be, respectively, 4 and 17 times that at present. Pursuing the international climate policy target of limiting global warming is projected to reduce exposure to floods in China. In this way, flood losses in China can be reduced by tens of billions of U.S. dollars (on average, US$67 billion and up to 0.04% of GDP) for each 0.5¬įC that warming is reduced. Our study improves understanding of the impact of climatic and nonclimatic changes on flood risk. Our scientific contribution is the first study to quantify flood impacts across China under different development pathways (shared socioeconomic pathways) for a broad range of global warming levels.

In the warming climate, flood risk is likely to increase over much of the globe. We present projections of changes of flood losses in China for a range of global warming scenarios, from 1.5¬į to 4.0¬įC above the preindustrial temperature, with a 0.5¬įC step. Projections of flood losses in China are based on river runoff simulations by a distributed hydrological model driven by multiple downscaled general circulation models, the national GDP projected at shared socioeconomic pathways, and the ‚Äúintensity‚Äďloss rate‚ÄĚ function. When interpreting changes caused by the combined effect of economic and climatic conditions, flood losses in China are projected to soar in the future, particularly in lowland regions subject to rapid economic growth. Under global warming of 1.5¬į and 4.0¬įC, in an average year, flood losses are projected to be, respectively, 4 and 17 times that at present. Pursuing the international climate policy target of limiting global warming is projected to reduce exposure to floods in China. In this way, flood losses in China can be reduced by tens of billions of U.S. dollars (on average, US$67 billion and up to 0.04% of GDP) for each 0.5¬įC that warming is reduced. Our study improves understanding of the impact of climatic and nonclimatic changes on flood risk. Our scientific contribution is the first study to quantify flood impacts across China under different development pathways (shared socioeconomic pathways) for a broad range of global warming levels.

Since climate models generate differing projections, ensembles of general circulation models (GCMs) are used. Their selection as well as the process of downscaling (empirical‚Äďstatistical or dynamic) can explain a major portion of differences in impact projections. Since climate models do not satisfactorily simulate the historical climate, a bias correction is often necessary and this is again a source of uncertainty. Finally, hydrological models‚ÄĒtheir structures, parameters, initial conditions, flood threshold estimation, and calibration methods‚ÄĒare also a source of further uncertainty.

Limiting global warming to no more than 2.0¬įC above the preindustrial level has been recognized as an important global climate policy target, and meeting this target may reduce disastrous consequences in comparison to continuing business as usual (Vautard et al. 2014). The Paris Agreement of the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) goes even further, proposing to hold the increase in global mean temperature to well below 2.0¬įC above preindustrial levels and to pursue efforts to limit the warming to 1.5¬įC (UNFCCC 2015).

Changes in flood hazard in a warming world can be illustrated by changes in the return period of what used to be 30-, 50-, and 100-yr events in the reference period 1981‚Äď2010. It is projected that historical reference 100-yr floods in China under 1.5¬į, 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC global warming scenarios will occur much more frequently, becoming 71- (interquartile range, IQR: 58‚Äď81), 68- (50‚Äď78), 59- (44‚Äď72), 50- (39‚Äď60), 42- (37‚Äď58) , and 42-yr (32‚Äď47) events (medians of GCM results), respectively. The average rate of decrease of the return period is 7 yr (0.5¬įC)[‚Äď1] from 1.5¬į to 4.0¬įC (Fig. 2g). In space, the return periods for reference 100-yr floods (determined for 1981‚Äď2010) are projected to become shorter over 80%, 78%, 80%, 82%, 81%, and 82% of China at the 1.5¬į, 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC global warming levels, respectively (Figs. 2a‚Äďf). Reference 100-yr events might become at least 5 times more frequent, that is, <20-yr floods under the 1.5¬į, 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC warming scenarios in 0.8%, 1.4%, 3.0%, 6.4%, 9.7%, and 15.5% of the area of China, respectively, mainly along a southwest-to-northeast belt crossing the Southwest, Upper and Middle Yangtze, Middle Yellow, Upper Haihe, western Liaohe, and eastern Songhua River basins. In most areas projected to encounter increasing floods, reference 100-yr events are projected to become 50‚Äď100-yr events under the 1.5¬į and 2.0¬įC warming scenarios and 20‚Äď50-yr events under the 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC warming scenarios. In contrast, floods are projected to become less severe in the Upper Songhua, Lower Liaohe, Upper Yellow, and northern Huaihe River basins.

evidence.section: The GDP in the area exposed to floods
evidence.original_text: With an increase in warming, GDP exposed to floods in China significantly increases, due to both the growth of economy and the increase of affected area (Fig. 3). Floods in China affected an area of 1.2 million km[2] yr[‚Äď1] in the reference period 1981‚Äď2010, and approximately $0.32 trillion of annual GDP was present in the affected area, accounting for 14% of national GDP. From the reference period (~0.7¬įC warmer than preindustrial conditions) to 4.0¬įC warming, the increase of flood-affected area is projected at the rate of 0.2 (IQR: 0.1‚Äď0.3) million km[2 ] per 0.5¬įC of warming, while national GDP is projected to increase by $9.0 (9.0‚Äď10.2) trillion per 0.5¬įC of warming. Under the 1.5¬į, 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC warming levels, the flood-affected area is projected to be 1.7 (1.5‚Äď2.1), 1.7 (1.5‚Äď2.2), 2.0 (1.6‚Äď2.5), 2.2 (1.8‚Äď2.6), 2.3 (2.0‚Äď2.8), and 2.4 (1.9‚Äď3.1) million km[2] , that is, 42%‚Äď100% higher than that in the reference period. The national GDP is projected to be $19.8 ($18.4‚Äď$24.6), $31.1 ($24.6‚Äď$38.0), $41.6 ($35.3‚Äď$48.8), $46.5 ($42.9‚Äď$55.7), $52.6 ($49.7‚Äď$60.6), and $64.3 ($57.3‚Äď$72.2) trillion, that is, 8.6‚Äď28.0 times higher than that in the reference period (Figs. 3a,b).

Runoff at the 0.5¬į gridcell scale is simulated using a distributed hydrological model, Variable Infiltration Capacity (VIC), at 214 tertiary watersheds in China (see Fig. ES1b). The parameters of the hydrological model were calibrated using the observed daily discharge data from 61 selected gauging stations (Tables ES2 and ES3). The parameters were transferred to neighboring catchments, based on the similarity of climate conditions, land cover, soil properties, digital elevation models, etc. Streamflow was projected until 2099, and the results for 1981‚Äď2010 and 2011‚Äď99 were used to represent the model reference period and future conditions, respectively.

Floods have been one of the key national concerns in China throughout its history, hence projections of increases in extreme precipitation (Qin et al. 2015) are bad news. A warmer climate is likely to lead to an expansion of area affected by severe floods (Hoegh-Guldberg et al. 2018). Limiting global warming to no more than 2.0¬įC above the preindustrial level has been recognized as an important global climate policy target, and meeting this target may reduce disastrous consequences in comparison to continuing business as usual (Vautard et al. 2014). The Paris Agreement of the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) goes even further, proposing to hold the increase in global mean temperature to well below 2.0¬įC above preindustrial levels and to pursue efforts to limit the warming to 1.5¬įC (UNFCCC 2015).

evidence.original_text: Flood losses can be quantified by combining the flood regime, the socioeconomic status, and the intensity‚Äďloss rate function representing the socioeconomic vulnerability to floods. To quantify socioeconomic vulnerability to floods in China, the ‚Äúintensity‚Äďloss rate‚ÄĚ curves were established for each tertiary watershed, based on the historical river runoff and direct economic losses for 1984‚Äď2018. Here, intensity of a flood event is quantified as to what extent maximum daily runoff fluctuated from its historical norm (1981‚Äď2010) by means of standard deviation over flooded grids in a tertiary watershed. The loss rate is the ratio between watershed flood losses and GDP in flood-exposed areas (see ‚ÄúIntensity‚Äďloss rate curve‚ÄĚ section in the supplemental material). Then, the intensity‚Äďloss rate combined with future flood exposure and projected flood intensity was used to estimate the future flood losses.

entries.head: doc_c3a0bf3c8379f7f1_c5

The Paris Agreement of the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) goes even further, proposing to hold the increase in global mean temperature to well below 2.0¬įC above preindustrial levels and to pursue efforts to limit the warming to 1.5¬įC (UNFCCC 2015).

evidence.original_text: Annual average flood losses recorded in the period 1984‚Äď2018 in China reached $19.2 billion (normalized to 2015 values), which accounted for 0.5% of the national GDP in China and 54% of the total national direct economic losses due to climate and weather. Yet, in the period 2006‚Äď18, flood losses increased to $25.3 billion annually. The most costly floods in a single year were recorded in 1998, 2010, and 2016, resulting in absolute losses of $49.7 billion, $64.6 billion, and $49.3 billion, respectively, that is, much more than double (in case of 2010, more than triple) the average of the 1984‚Äď2018 period (Fig. 1a). The destructive floods in 1998 alone, which affected vast areas of China, including drainage basins of the Yangtze River, the Songhua River in northeast China, and the Pearl River in south China, consumed nearly 3.0% of the national GDP (China Meteorological Administration 2019). In spatial terms, the bulk of high flood losses are in the lowland plain areas with high damage potential. Areas at elevation below 200 m MSL (Fig. 1b) constitute 15% of China, but flood losses in these areas make up half of the national total, on average, and may even reach at 70% in some particular years, such as 1991 and 1998 (Fig. ES5).

entries.name: Historical flood losses and GDP data for China (1984-2018)

evidence.original_text: Observed climate records from more than 2400 stations for the period 1961‚Äď2018 were collected from the National Meteorological Information Center of the China Meteorological Administration and hydrological records from 61 stations for the period 1961‚Äď2012 come from Hydrological Yearbooks compiled by the Hydrological Bureau of Ministry of Water Resources, China.

entries.name: Observed climate and hydrological records for China (1961-2018)

evidence.original_text: County-level socioeconomic data for the period 1984‚Äď2018 were collected from the China Statistical Yearbook (County-Level) (e.g., National Bureau of Statistics of China 2019) and interpolated to 0.5¬į resolution by the area-weighted interpolation method. To maintain the homogeneity of the data series, losses and GDP records for 1984‚Äď2018 were normalized to 2015 prices.

The provincial-scale socioeconomy for 2010‚Äď2100 is projected under the SSPs parameter scheme (Leimbach et al. 2017) by considering the regional information on total factor productivity, capital stock, and labor force from the latest census and the current universal two-child policy in China for labor force projection (Jiang et al. 2018; Huang et al. 2019). Gridded GDP is derived by scaling the SSP projections to 0.5¬į resolution, based on the weights of individual grid cells to total provincial GDP, which is from recorded data. The projected GDP was normalized to 2015 prices.

entries.name: Projected gridded GDP under SSPs for China (2010-2100)

evidence.section: Flood losses under different warming scenarios
evidence.original_text: Assuming socioeconomic futures after SSPs, one can project flood losses to increase significantly at the 1.5¬įC warming level, with average annual losses of $112 billion (IQR: $91‚Äď$136 billion), that is, more than 4 times higher than the losses in the recent period of 2006‚Äď18, amounting to $25.3 billion (Table ES5). Increasing changes are projected for further warming, with the average rate reaching $67 ($62‚Äď$82) billion per 0.5¬įC increment. The lowest increase, $44 billion, is projected between 1.5¬į and 2.0¬įC of warming and the highest, $106 billion, between 3.0¬į and 3.5¬įC of warmning. Further warming is projected to lead to flood losses of $156 ($123‚Äď$193), $221 ($178‚Äď$278), $274 ($238‚Äď$365), $380 ($342‚Äď$456), and $435 ($385‚Äď$534) billion under the 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC global warming scenarios, respectively (Fig. 4a). Annual flood losses in relation to GDP reached, on average, 0.92% in 1984‚Äď2005, while the value of this index decreased to 0.28% for the more recent period of 2006‚Äď18, due to dynamic economic development (China Meteorological Administration 2019). However, the declining trend of relative GDP loss may reverse in the future with persistent warming. Flood-induced losses are likely to consume approximately 0.53% (IQR: 0.46%‚Äď0.65%) of GDP at the 1.5¬įC warming level, while the 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC warming levels are 0.48% (0.40%‚Äď0.61%), 0.52% (0.42%‚Äď0.66%), 0.61% (0.48%‚Äď0.77%), 0.66% (0.57%‚Äď0.91%), and 0.71% (0.58%‚Äď0.91%) of GDP, respectively (Fig. 4b). For each 0.5¬įC warming increment, up to 0.04% (0.03%‚Äď0.07%) of the growing GDP is projected to be consumed, additionally, by floods.

entries.name: Projected flood losses and GDP exposure under global warming scenarios

evidence.original_text: To maintain the homogeneity of the data series, losses and GDP records for 1984‚Äď2018 were normalized to 2015 prices.

entries.description: Data normalization to 2015 prices for homogeneity

evidence.original_text: Referring to the observational data, GCM outputs were downscaled and bias-corrected to 0.5¬į resolution (see ‚ÄúSelected climate models‚ÄĚ section in the supplemental material).

entries.description: Climate model outputs downscaled and bias-corrected to 0.5¬į resolution

evidence.original_text: County-level socioeconomic data for the period 1984‚Äď2018 were collected from the China Statistical Yearbook (County-Level) (e.g., National Bureau of Statistics of China 2019) and interpolated to 0.5¬į resolution by the area-weighted interpolation method.

entries.description: County-level socioeconomic data interpolated to 0.5¬į resolution using area-weighted method

evidence.original_text: Gridded GDP is derived by scaling the SSP projections to 0.5¬į resolution, based on the weights of individual grid cells to total provincial GDP, which is from recorded data.

entries.description: Gridded GDP derived by scaling SSP projections to 0.5¬į resolution based on provincial GDP weights

evidence.original_text: For the convenience of analysis, flood events at each tertiary watershed are identified according to flood thresholds, which are defined at grid scale as the mean plus one standard deviation (SD) of annual maximum runoff for 1981‚Äď2010.

entries.description: Flood thresholds defined as mean plus one standard deviation of annual maximum runoff for reference period

evidence.original_text: The thresholds of 30-, 50-, or 100-yr floods at each grid were estimated for the reference period (1981‚Äď2010) using the generalized extreme value (GEV) distribution function (Kotz and Nadarajah 2000).

entries.description: Thresholds of extreme floods estimated using Generalized Extreme Value (GEV) distribution function

evidence.original_text: Timings of the warming by 1.5¬į, 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC above the preindustrial level are obtained using 29-yr running-average global mean surface temperature for each model (12 GCMs with 21 runs) and RCP scenario (three RCPs: RCP2.6, RCP4.5, and RCP8.5). The warming periods are defined as the 29-yr windows centered on the years when global warming levels are exceeded (Tables ES6‚ÄďES8).

entries.description: Warming periods defined as 29-year windows centered on years when global warming levels are exceeded

evidence.original_text: Runoff at the 0.5¬į gridcell scale is simulated using a distributed hydrological model, Variable Infiltration Capacity (VIC), at 214 tertiary watersheds in China (see Fig. ES1b). The parameters of the hydrological model were calibrated using the observed daily discharge data from 61 selected gauging stations (Tables ES2 and ES3). The parameters were transferred to neighboring catchments, based on the similarity of climate conditions, land cover, soil properties, digital elevation models, etc. Streamflow was projected until 2099, and the results for 1981‚Äď2010 and 2011‚Äď99 were used to represent the model reference period and future conditions, respectively.

For the convenience of analysis, flood events at each tertiary watershed are identified according to flood thresholds, which are defined at grid scale as the mean plus one standard deviation (SD) of annual maximum runoff for 1981‚Äď2010. Acceptance of this particular definition of flood threshold clearly affects the quantitative results of analysis. The thresholds of 30-, 50-, or 100-yr floods at each grid were estimated for the reference period (1981‚Äď2010) using the generalized extreme value (GEV) distribution function (Kotz and Nadarajah 2000).

Flood losses can be quantified by combining the flood regime, the socioeconomic status, and the intensity‚Äďloss rate function representing the socioeconomic vulnerability to floods. To quantify socioeconomic vulnerability to floods in China, the ‚Äúintensity‚Äďloss rate‚ÄĚ curves were established for each tertiary watershed, based on the historical river runoff and direct economic losses for 1984‚Äď2018. Here, intensity of a flood event is quantified as to what extent maximum daily runoff fluctuated from its historical norm (1981‚Äď2010) by means of standard deviation over flooded grids in a tertiary watershed. The loss rate is the ratio between watershed flood losses and GDP in flood-exposed areas (see ‚ÄúIntensity‚Äďloss rate curve‚ÄĚ section in the supplemental material). Then, the intensity‚Äďloss rate combined with future flood exposure and projected flood intensity was used to estimate the future flood losses.

County-level socioeconomic data for the period 1984‚Äď2018 were collected from the China Statistical Yearbook (County-Level) (e.g., National Bureau of Statistics of China 2019) and interpolated to 0.5¬į resolution by the area-weighted interpolation method. To maintain the homogeneity of the data series, losses and GDP records for 1984‚Äď2018 were normalized to 2015 prices.

The 21 climate simulations used to drive the hydrological model in this study are outputs from 12 GCMs, namely, CNRM-CM5, CanESM2, GFDL-CM3, GFDL-ESM2G, GFDL-ESM2M, HadGEM2-ES, IPSL-CM5A-LR, MIROC-ESM, MIROC-ESM-CHEM, NorESM1-M, MPI-ESM-MR, and MPI-ESM-LR with different runs (see Table ES1). The climate variables include precipitation, temperature (mean, maximum, minimum), relative humidity, wind speed, and surface downwelling shortwave radiation. Referring to the observational data, GCM outputs were downscaled and bias-corrected to 0.5¬į resolution (see ‚ÄúSelected climate models‚ÄĚ section in the supplemental material). Five SSPs were designed to represent different strategies of socioeconomic development and challenges of mitigation and adaptation (O‚ÄôNeill et al. 2014). Considering the socioeconomic challenges to mitigation and economic development under different SSPs (O‚ÄôNeill et al. 2017; Huang et al. 2019), the ‚ÄúSustainability‚ÄĚ pathway SSP1 with a low challenge to mitigation, ‚ÄúMiddle of the road‚ÄĚ SSP2 with a medium challenge to mitigation, and ‚ÄúFossil-fueled development‚ÄĚ pathway SSP5 with a high challenge to mitigation are separately combined with the RCP2.6, RCP4.5, and RCP8.5 scenarios to assess flood impacts at global warming levels of 1.5¬į, 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC. The provincial-scale socioeconomy for 2010‚Äď2100 is projected under the SSPs parameter scheme (Leimbach et al. 2017) by considering the regional information on total factor productivity, capital stock, and labor force from the latest census and the current universal two-child policy in China for labor force projection (Jiang et al. 2018; Huang et al. 2019). Gridded GDP is derived by scaling the SSP projections to 0.5¬į resolution, based on the weights of individual grid cells to total provincial GDP, which is from recorded data. The projected GDP was normalized to 2015 prices.

entries.task: projection of flood losses under different global warming scenarios
entries.setup: 21 GCM simulations from CMIP5, VIC hydrological model, SSP socioeconomic scenarios, intensity-loss rate function

evidence.original_text: Definitions of 1.5¬į, 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC warmer world. Timings of the warming by 1.5¬į, 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC above the preindustrial level are obtained using 29-yr running-average global mean surface temperature for each model (12 GCMs with 21 runs) and RCP scenario (three RCPs: RCP2.6, RCP4.5, and RCP8.5). The warming periods are defined as the 29-yr windows centered on the years when global warming levels are exceeded (Tables ES6‚ÄďES8). All results under different warming levels are first calculated by the average of changes in the corresponding warming periods for each climate model and RCP scenario, and then obtained from the median of multimodel ensemble with different RCP scenarios. Uncertainties from different data sources are shown by the multimodel interquartile range.

entries.task: definition of warming periods and calculation of results
entries.setup: 29-yr running-average global mean surface temperature, multimodel ensemble

evidence.section: Flood losses under different warming scenarios
evidence.original_text: Assuming socioeconomic futures after SSPs, one can project flood losses to increase significantly at the 1.5¬įC warming level, with average annual losses of $112 billion (IQR: $91‚Äď$136 billion), that is, more than 4 times higher than the losses in the recent period of 2006‚Äď18, amounting to $25.3 billion (Table ES5). Increasing changes are projected for further warming, with the average rate reaching $67 ($62‚Äď$82) billion per 0.5¬įC increment. The lowest increase, $44 billion, is projected between 1.5¬į and 2.0¬įC of warming and the highest, $106 billion, between 3.0¬į and 3.5¬įC of warmning. Further warming is projected to lead to flood losses of $156 ($123‚Äď$193), $221 ($178‚Äď$278), $274 ($238‚Äď$365), $380 ($342‚Äď$456), and $435 ($385‚Äď$534) billion under the 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC global warming scenarios, respectively (Fig. 4a). Annual flood losses in relation to GDP reached, on average, 0.92% in 1984‚Äď2005, while the value of this index decreased to 0.28% for the more recent period of 2006‚Äď18, due to dynamic economic development (China Meteorological Administration 2019). However, the declining trend of relative GDP loss may reverse in the future with persistent warming. Flood-induced losses are likely to consume approximately 0.53% (IQR: 0.46%‚Äď0.65%) of GDP at the 1.5¬įC warming level, while the 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC warming levels are 0.48% (0.40%‚Äď0.61%), 0.52% (0.42%‚Äď0.66%), 0.61% (0.48%‚Äď0.77%), 0.66% (0.57%‚Äď0.91%), and 0.71% (0.58%‚Äď0.91%) of GDP, respectively (Fig. 4b). For each 0.5¬įC warming increment, up to 0.04% (0.03%‚Äď0.07%) of the growing GDP is projected to be consumed, additionally, by floods.

entries.context: China at 1.5¬įC global warming level, based on socioeconomic futures after SSPs, median of 21 GCM-driven results

evidence.original_text: It is projected that historical reference 100-yr floods in China under 1.5¬į, 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC global warming scenarios will occur much more frequently, becoming 71- (interquartile range, IQR: 58‚Äď81), 68- (50‚Äď78), 59- (44‚Äď72), 50- (39‚Äď60), 42- (37‚Äď58) , and 42-yr (32‚Äď47) events (medians of GCM results), respectively. The average rate of decrease of the return period is 7 yr (0.5¬įC)[‚Äď1] from 1.5¬į to 4.0¬įC (Fig. 2g). In space, the return periods for reference 100-yr floods (determined for 1981‚Äď2010) are projected to become shorter over 80%, 78%, 80%, 82%, 81%, and 82% of China at the 1.5¬į, 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC global warming levels, respectively (Figs. 2a‚Äďf). Reference 100-yr events might become at least 5 times more frequent, that is, <20-yr floods under the 1.5¬į, 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC warming scenarios in 0.8%, 1.4%, 3.0%, 6.4%, 9.7%, and 15.5% of the area of China, respectively, mainly along a southwest-to-northeast belt crossing the Southwest, Upper and Middle Yangtze, Middle Yellow, Upper Haihe, western Liaohe, and eastern Songhua River basins. In most areas projected to encounter increasing floods, reference 100-yr events are projected to become 50‚Äď100-yr events under the 1.5¬į and 2.0¬įC warming scenarios and 20‚Äď50-yr events under the 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC warming scenarios. In contrast, floods are projected to become less severe in the Upper Songhua, Lower Liaohe, Upper Yellow, and northern Huaihe River basins.

entries.context: China under 1.5¬įC global warming scenario, median of GCM results

evidence.section: The GDP in the area exposed to floods
evidence.original_text: With an increase in warming, GDP exposed to floods in China significantly increases, due to both the growth of economy and the increase of affected area (Fig. 3). Floods in China affected an area of 1.2 million km[2] yr[‚Äď1] in the reference period 1981‚Äď2010, and approximately $0.32 trillion of annual GDP was present in the affected area, accounting for 14% of national GDP. From the reference period (~0.7¬įC warmer than preindustrial conditions) to 4.0¬įC warming, the increase of flood-affected area is projected at the rate of 0.2 (IQR: 0.1‚Äď0.3) million km[2 ] per 0.5¬įC of warming, while national GDP is projected to increase by $9.0 (9.0‚Äď10.2) trillion per 0.5¬įC of warming. Under the 1.5¬į, 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC warming levels, the flood-affected area is projected to be 1.7 (1.5‚Äď2.1), 1.7 (1.5‚Äď2.2), 2.0 (1.6‚Äď2.5), 2.2 (1.8‚Äď2.6), 2.3 (2.0‚Äď2.8), and 2.4 (1.9‚Äď3.1) million km[2] , that is, 42%‚Äď100% higher than that in the reference period. The national GDP is projected to be $19.8 ($18.4‚Äď$24.6), $31.1 ($24.6‚Äď$38.0), $41.6 ($35.3‚Äď$48.8), $46.5 ($42.9‚Äď$55.7), $52.6 ($49.7‚Äď$60.6), and $64.3 ($57.3‚Äď$72.2) trillion, that is, 8.6‚Äď28.0 times higher than that in the reference period (Figs. 3a,b). With the increase in affected area to 2.4 (IQR: 1.9‚Äď3.1) million km[2] (Fig. 3b) and the growth of the economy to $64.3 ($57.3‚Äď$72.2) trillion (Fig. 3a) at the 4.0¬įC warming level, increasing wealth is projected to be accumulated in the flood-exposed area (Fig. 3c). From reference period to 4.0¬įC, the increase of flood exposure is $2.2 ($2.0‚Äď$2.5) trillion per 0.5¬įC of warming. The GDP in the exposed area is projected to increase to $4.4 ($3.8‚Äď$5.1), $5.9 ($4.9‚Äď$6.8), $7.9 ($7.0‚Äď$10.0), $10.3 ($8.7‚Äď$11.8), $12.8 ($11.5‚Äď$14.3), and $14.8 ($13.7‚Äď$16.9) trillion for the 1.5¬į, 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC global warming scenarios, respectively, which are 14 (12‚Äď 16), 18 (15‚Äď21), 25 (22‚Äď31), 32 (27‚Äď37), 40 (36‚Äď45), and 46 (43‚Äď53) times that in the reference period.

entries.context: China under 1.5¬įC global warming scenario, median of 21 GCM-driven results

evidence.section: Flood losses under different warming scenarios
evidence.original_text: Assuming socioeconomic futures after SSPs, one can project flood losses to increase significantly at the 1.5¬įC warming level, with average annual losses of $112 billion (IQR: $91‚Äď$136 billion), that is, more than 4 times higher than the losses in the recent period of 2006‚Äď18, amounting to $25.3 billion (Table ES5). Increasing changes are projected for further warming, with the average rate reaching $67 ($62‚Äď$82) billion per 0.5¬įC increment. The lowest increase, $44 billion, is projected between 1.5¬į and 2.0¬įC of warming and the highest, $106 billion, between 3.0¬į and 3.5¬įC of warmning. Further warming is projected to lead to flood losses of $156 ($123‚Äď$193), $221 ($178‚Äď$278), $274 ($238‚Äď$365), $380 ($342‚Äď$456), and $435 ($385‚Äď$534) billion under the 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC global warming scenarios, respectively (Fig. 4a). Annual flood losses in relation to GDP reached, on average, 0.92% in 1984‚Äď2005, while the value of this index decreased to 0.28% for the more recent period of 2006‚Äď18, due to dynamic economic development (China Meteorological Administration 2019). However, the declining trend of relative GDP loss may reverse in the future with persistent warming. Flood-induced losses are likely to consume approximately 0.53% (IQR: 0.46%‚Äď0.65%) of GDP at the 1.5¬įC warming level, while the 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC warming levels are 0.48% (0.40%‚Äď0.61%), 0.52% (0.42%‚Äď0.66%), 0.61% (0.48%‚Äď0.77%), 0.66% (0.57%‚Äď0.91%), and 0.71% (0.58%‚Äď0.91%) of GDP, respectively (Fig. 4b). For each 0.5¬įC warming increment, up to 0.04% (0.03%‚Äď0.07%) of the growing GDP is projected to be consumed, additionally, by floods.

It is projected that historical reference 100-yr floods in China under 1.5¬į, 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC global warming scenarios will occur much more frequently, becoming 71- (interquartile range, IQR: 58‚Äď81), 68- (50‚Äď78), 59- (44‚Äď72), 50- (39‚Äď60), 42- (37‚Äď58) , and 42-yr (32‚Äď47) events (medians of GCM results), respectively. The average rate of decrease of the return period is 7 yr (0.5¬įC)[‚Äď1] from 1.5¬į to 4.0¬įC (Fig. 2g). In space, the return periods for reference 100-yr floods (determined for 1981‚Äď2010) are projected to become shorter over 80%, 78%, 80%, 82%, 81%, and 82% of China at the 1.5¬į, 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC global warming levels, respectively (Figs. 2a‚Äďf). Reference 100-yr events might become at least 5 times more frequent, that is, <20-yr floods under the 1.5¬į, 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC warming scenarios in 0.8%, 1.4%, 3.0%, 6.4%, 9.7%, and 15.5% of the area of China, respectively, mainly along a southwest-to-northeast belt crossing the Southwest, Upper and Middle Yangtze, Middle Yellow, Upper Haihe, western Liaohe, and eastern Songhua River basins. In most areas projected to encounter increasing floods, reference 100-yr events are projected to become 50‚Äď100-yr events under the 1.5¬į and 2.0¬įC warming scenarios and 20‚Äď50-yr events under the 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC warming scenarios. In contrast, floods are projected to become less severe in the Upper Songhua, Lower Liaohe, Upper Yellow, and northern Huaihe River basins.

In the warming climate, flood risk is likely to increase over much of the globe. We present projections of changes of flood losses in China for a range of global warming scenarios, from 1.5¬į to 4.0¬įC above the preindustrial temperature, with a 0.5¬įC step. Projections of flood losses in China are based on river runoff simulations by a distributed hydrological model driven by multiple downscaled general circulation models, the national GDP projected at shared socioeconomic pathways, and the ‚Äúintensity‚Äďloss rate‚ÄĚ function. When interpreting changes caused by the combined effect of economic and climatic conditions, flood losses in China are projected to soar in the future, particularly in lowland regions subject to rapid economic growth. Under global warming of 1.5¬į and 4.0¬įC, in an average year, flood losses are projected to be, respectively, 4 and 17 times that at present. Pursuing the international climate policy target of limiting global warming is projected to reduce exposure to floods in China. In this way, flood losses in China can be reduced by tens of billions of U.S. dollars (on average, US$67 billion and up to 0.04% of GDP) for each 0.5¬įC that warming is reduced. Our study improves understanding of the impact of climatic and nonclimatic changes on flood risk. Our scientific contribution is the first study to quantify flood impacts across China under different development pathways (shared socioeconomic pathways) for a broad range of global warming levels.

Along with the increasing flood hazard, also exposure to floods is projected to increase with global warming. As a result, the severity of flood impacts is likely to increase throughout China under warming climate. The GDP that is exposed to floods is projected to increase by a factor of almost 14 (IQR: 12‚Äď16) and 46 (43‚Äď53) relative to 1981‚Äď2010 levels, respectively, at the 1.5¬į and 4.0¬įC warming scenarios. When interpreting changes caused by the combined effect of economic and climatic conditions, flood losses are projected to be higher than those during the historical period by 4‚Äď17 times under 1.5¬į‚Äď4.0¬įC of global warming, with greater impacts in lowland regions. Projections of floods based on multi-GCM-driven simulations have moderately high consistency. More areas are expected to be under the risk of frequent and intense floods in a warmer climate. Approximately 0.2 (IQR: 0.1‚Äď0.3) million km[2] of exposed area and $2.2 ($2.0‚Äď$2.5) trillion of economic exposure to floods could be reduced for each 0.5¬įC of less warming. Thus, pursuing the target of limiting global warming is projected to reduce flood losses in China by tens of billions of U.S. dollars for each 0.5¬įC of less warming, especially for the flood-prone Yangtze River basin. We examined the policy-relevant range of warming, from 1.5¬į to 4.0¬įC above preindustrial levels, and found that each additional warming by 0.5¬įC is projected to increase the flood loss by $67 ($62‚Äď$82) billion, on average. Huge increase of flood losses under the warming climate is largely from the socioeconomic conditions, but still can be also attributed to the increase of flood frequency and intensity. Under the assumption of fixed economic condition at the year 2010, flood losses would still increase by 32%‚Äď125% under 1.5¬į‚Äď4.0¬įC of global warming. Pursuing a more stringent warming target allows us to reduce the index of relative loss as the share of GDP. Up to 0.06% (IQR: 0.05%‚Äď0.09%) of GDP could be saved by pursuing the 0.5¬įC of reduced warming under the assumption of a frozen economy at the level of year 2010. When considering the rapid growth of the economy in China, this figure goes down to 0.04% (0.03%‚Äď0.07%).

For the convenience of analysis, flood events at each tertiary watershed are identified according to flood thresholds, which are defined at grid scale as the mean plus one standard deviation (SD) of annual maximum runoff for 1981‚Äď2010. Acceptance of this particular definition of flood threshold clearly affects the quantitative results of analysis. The thresholds of 30-, 50-, or 100-yr floods at each grid were estimated for the reference period (1981‚Äď2010) using the generalized extreme value (GEV) distribution function (Kotz and Nadarajah 2000).

The 21 climate simulations used to drive the hydrological model in this study are outputs from 12 GCMs, namely, CNRM-CM5, CanESM2, GFDL-CM3, GFDL-ESM2G, GFDL-ESM2M, HadGEM2-ES, IPSL-CM5A-LR, MIROC-ESM, MIROC-ESM-CHEM, NorESM1-M, MPI-ESM-MR, and MPI-ESM-LR with different runs (see Table ES1). The climate variables include precipitation, temperature (mean, maximum, minimum), relative humidity, wind speed, and surface downwelling shortwave radiation. Referring to the observational data, GCM outputs were downscaled and bias-corrected to 0.5¬į resolution (see ‚ÄúSelected climate models‚ÄĚ section in the supplemental material).

Five SSPs were designed to represent different strategies of socioeconomic development and challenges of mitigation and adaptation (O‚ÄôNeill et al. 2014). Considering the socioeconomic challenges to mitigation and economic development under different SSPs (O‚ÄôNeill et al. 2017; Huang et al. 2019), the ‚ÄúSustainability‚ÄĚ pathway SSP1 with a low challenge to mitigation, ‚ÄúMiddle of the road‚ÄĚ SSP2 with a medium challenge to mitigation, and ‚ÄúFossil-fueled development‚ÄĚ pathway SSP5 with a high challenge to mitigation are separately combined with the RCP2.6, RCP4.5, and RCP8.5 scenarios to assess flood impacts at global warming levels of 1.5¬į, 2.0¬į, 2.5¬į, 3.0¬į, 3.5¬į, and 4.0¬įC. The provincial-scale socioeconomy for 2010‚Äď2100 is projected under the SSPs parameter scheme (Leimbach et al. 2017) by considering the regional information on total factor productivity, capital stock, and labor force from the latest census and the current universal two-child policy in China for labor force projection (Jiang et al. 2018; Huang et al. 2019). Gridded GDP is derived by scaling the SSP projections to 0.5¬į resolution, based on the weights of individual grid cells to total provincial GDP, which is from recorded data. The projected GDP was normalized to 2015 prices.

evidence.section: Insights into flood mitigation and risk reduction in China in a changing climate
evidence.original_text: Our findings are policy relevant, illustrating that massive adaptation to changing conditions will be required to reduce future river flood risk in China. This is of considerable potential relevance and interest to policy- and decision-makers, both in the field of climate change adaptation and natural disaster reduction. Indeed, China has embarked upon an ambitious, high-budget task to upgrade flood-risk reduction measures and strengthen flood preparedness, by both structural and nonstructural measures that alleviate the burden of river flooding, but also flash and urban flooding. Flood-risk reduction is one of the principal climate change adaptation activities in China, as discussed in more detail in Kundzewicz et al. (2019).
